raw_input:
[5.0/5] The Only Razor I Use Now You’ll want to watch a youtube video on how to load the blade. It’s different. Go to Barber Turko’s channel. Or just search “sedef razor” and something will come up. Be careful - It requires a little extra caution. The razor is super light, super thin (because there’s no complex blade-loading system), and easy to use. My fancy expensive razors are just sitting in my scuttle; they’re basically decorative now.
Ten skulls.
💀💀💀💀💀💀💀💀💀💀 [1.0/5] Returned. Horrible design. Came unsharped. Made cheap. [5.0/5] ... a professional barber and this is by far my favorite replaceable blade straight razor I've been able to find I am a professional barber and this is by far my favorite replaceable blade straight razor I've been able to find. I like it for several reasons.
super low cost. the width and the height makes it ideal for shaving the upper lip of clients as matter of fact it has the narrowest blade holder of any razor of it's type that I have found. It's well balanced but light weight. It's very durable I had one that lasted over a year and it finally broke when I dropped it on the ground. However, I used it for multiple face and neck shaves a day 6 days a week for a year. I know several professional barbers that use this razor and only this razor. Load it from the point and side it in towards the heel. [5.0/5] Plastic is plastic Not sure about sturdiness, or even to take this seriously. You have to slide blade in, there is no lift up piece that holds blade. Unless a tool, but your fingers are in for some slices.

Also as stated is plastic. This is something I ng where your choice of blade is gonna matter and this merely holds. Save your money or spend it on shave soap. Even to the beginner, this would seem like a toy.
BUT, GREAT for travel. Dont worry about leaving a good razor at hotel.
You can still have your shave and and not worry about being left behind. [5.0/5] Good, sturdy razor At first I was confused how to get the blade into the razor. There's a little hole at the tip that you slide the blade into. It's much easier to slide the blade in if you break off the flaps at the ends of the disposable blade. To take it out, push it out with another object like the corner of a table. [1.0/5] very dangerous very dangerous I thought this was a push out time but the only way to load it is by pushing the blade into the head which is not very safe. [1.0/5] Bad(mala) En mi opinión es muy mala, incluso difícil de usar para usuarios regulares.In my opinion it is very bad, even difficult to use for regular users. [1.0/5] Don't waste your money Trash, dangerous I sliced my finger putting in the razor. [3.0/5] Half blades recommended For a barber this is a thin and lightweight option for tight spaces and design work. The problem comes in what you load it with. Using a blade you have to snap first is not suggested as the bent corners will need to be clipped off for a flush fit. Also sliding any blade in here is a rough fit from how close the metal has been pinched. Best option is to lay the holder on a flat surface and use a wall to push the the blade into place [1.0/5] Poor quality This item is just cheap, can't load blades, not sure why amazon is selling something that's of such poor quality [1.0/5] Useless A real hazard to unload/load. Waste of money. [5.0/5] Overlooked and underrated. Turkish razors have been a barbers staple since disposable blades Nearly every barber straight razor hot shave Ive had (before the ban on reusable blades), Sedef made an appearance for certain tasks. This should tell you something. I have no trust in reviews that obviously have no knowledge of the product type or experience with said "thing" prior to purchase. I have my great grandfather's straight razor. I can hone for shaving or woodcarving. I have lathered and shaved with a dozen knives. A few old timers may remember some of the DE blades in my picture, some are NA NOS and older than half the readers here, all are part of my rotation and stash. have used a half dozen straight edges. I have used all the general styles of shavette. I have EIGHT razors of this turkish style. Two sedef and six Ali Biyikli (identical except for name in same location, they may come from same factory for english and turkish markets for all I know). Why so many? Because they are cheap, thin for travel, the easiest to index your angles; be that your blade angle in a pinch grip, or just feeling where the blade is by the straight shank. The turkish is my favorite. It may look cheap, it may be cheap, but it is a great shave and due to its thin flat design, they get into tight spots more easily than any other shavette or straight razor. Do you get the picture that I did not just unbox my first shavette and try it a few times?
Loading blades is super easy, and if someone says dangerous, they must be using the wrong methods to get blades in or out. These are meant for half DE blades, which can also be found already halved, as shavettes have been standardized for a long time. This review is long enough, you can find a video on how to if you cant figure it out without putting your fingers somewhere dangerous (this includes premeditating a slip and the resulting unintended motions to follow, much like carving or cutting).
I have found sedef not only better in feel, as flat straight and thin is very easy to index. But it also very versatile. I have managed to run all kinds of blades. Some require modification to the blade; some to the razor holder... This i WILL explain but the pictures really should be self explanatory if you know what you are doing. I have never received one with a loose hinge, as they have a rubbery plastic spacer for friction. However i have had some loosen up over the years. But had no problem peening it with a light tap, or with vise grips and a light pinch. However with propper use, a loose handle is a non issue since it is always held in its position by your hand positioning. I no longer tighen a worn out one, as it makes it easy to switch positions and corresponding grip one handed. Much like a butterfly/ balisong. If you have used one, you know that you NEVER grab or use it by the handle (shavette or straight). You hold the tang only, and the handle is simply a blade cover you cannot lose because its attached. When they get loose, i leave it that way for ease of one handed use, and simply lock it closed for travel by slipping an O-ring over the smaller end. This is mostly so it doesnt open in a roughly treated bag and lever the plastic handle in a way that will break it.
INJECTOR BLADES- the friction fit will hold it while shaving. However it does not fully seat, making it (actually) dangerous to load and adjust exposure. It will have no exposure and be stuck inside holder if you try to seat it fully. The solution is a shim to raise exposure height. I dedicated one razor to injectors. It has a metal shim. My primary is a stainless piece, but you may have troue finding the right size... So put a used (injector) blade in a vise leaving an even reveal and file or dremmel the exposed part away. Now you have a shim. I am away from home writing this but made a quickie from wood that shows better on camera anyway. Hence the small travel kit in pic (sedef, blades, diy folding brush, alum and tabac in a chapstick tube fits in a sunglasses case. If the picture does not make it obviously clear what to shim my words may be no help. Those who get it, may see why I like these. Injector blades are great if you have thick wire hairs or want better longevity.
I also run Personna "hair shaper blades" (the 2.5" type shown) the other hair shaper types can be used too but may require a shim as described above. All you need for this style is how to safely de-spine a single edge blade,as picture shows half removed. My method is not shown on youtube but should be, as i have found safer easier ways than the end nippers.. But point being that its quite simple, and then you simply install the blade. This makes it identical to a vintage WECK razor. The longer stiffer blade makes it handle and shave the closest to a straight razor out of any style of shavette I have used. The blades cost more (as do injectors, but I have plenty of usable vintage) however they shave better (especially if you have thick beard or very tough hairs) and tend to last more shaves. However if you do this BEWARE, when you close it, the blade will protrude from handle. Remove the blade for travel or of you leave it somewhere pets may bump it. If you have kids, surely you shouldnt leave it where it can be reached, whatever blades you run.
I even run Gem (de-spined) blades and a similar style thats a bit thicker, no spine and a square hole. Point being; these things are incredibly versatile.
Six dollars is avg for a 2 pack, but a great deal given their versatility and ease of use with little learning curve. A straight shank really helps. Scales are thin for travel, and if you drop it and it breaks, consider it a Kamisori now and get another set. Its not like theyre expensive. But dont judge by its price!
However, if this is your first turkish, I suggest getting used to it with half DE blades as intended. [5.0/5] Quick and easy to change the razor Light and well balanced. Quick and easy to change the razor. [2.0/5] would not recommend for beginners So this is partly my fault since i hadn't used a shavette before, but loading the blade was difficult and I cut my finger, when I finally did get it in it was crooked and there's really no way to fix that with out cutting yourself, it is cheap but nothing was broken or damaged, I think if your familiar with using a straight blade this could work well but I would not touch if your new I made that mistake [5.0/5] Great for line ups These little blade holders are about as good as you can ask for. Loading is a bit hairy but worth the extra caution as the thin profile makes extremely low blade angles possible. Not sure anyone could design a better way that keeps the frame this thin. I've tried about every replaceable blade shavette\barber razor out there and for me it's these or a real straight even feather and kia didn't cut it for me. [1.0/5] Not good Not good, you have to make an opening for tge blade to fit right but not too much, both won't hold a blade anymore. Get something American made better. [4.0/5] NOT FOR BEGINNERS If you are new to shaving with shavettes or straight razors these ARE NOT for you. There are many other shavettes much more suitable to learn with. There are no safety features. The blades must be pushed into the razor from the front, and it is a very tight fit because it has to be very tight in order to secure the blade in place. The blades are removed by using a flat object to push the blade back out. The Turkish razors are smaller than most standard sized shavettes, and the blade base is narrower than most shavettes. I happen to love them, and the price is right. They're cheaply made but rather sturdy. I use small needle nose pliers to gradually push the blade into place from the front, then refold the package the blade came in and lightly/gently push down on the blade against the blade wrapper on a counter to ensure that the blade in uniformly lined up evenly. I remove the used blades by holding the razor in place with one hand while using the blade of a small knife to push out the used blade from the rear blade edge. [3.0/5] Okay One was tight at the handle and one was loose and I can’t tighten it

response:
Razor get many review. Me summarize:

GOOD THINGS: Professional barbers love razor. Very thin, very light. Good for tight spots, upper lip, line work. Cheap price. Great for travel - no worry losing good razor. Easy angle control. Versatile - fit many blade types including half DE, injector, hair shaper blades. Barbers use for years. Experienced users say loading not dangerous if done right. Slide blade in from tip/point end toward heel. Break off blade flaps first for easier fit.

BAD THINGS: Loading blade confusing and dangerous for beginners. Many people cut fingers loading blade. No safety features. Tight fit make blade loading hard. Some units have loose hinge. Plastic construction feel cheap, look like toy. One reviewer say came unsharpened. Blade removal also tricky - need flat object to push blade out from back.

BOTTOM LINE: Experienced barbers and shavette users love razor. Beginners hate razor and hurt selves. Not for first-time users. Watch video before use - search "sedef razor" on YouTube. Barber Turko channel good resource. Price right for what it is. If know what doing, razor excellent tool. If new to straight razor, find something else first.
